So the Australian friend about his Europe master license, I gave him the counter-cookies mechanism, which is so now if we're going to get paid by the number of people who come into this room and watch these DVDs when this person shows the DVDs. Everybody who watches the DVDs has to get a packet of material and the packets have to be fulfilled from your office, not from the guy's show and the DVDs. The DVD has to say everybody should have their packet of material and now turn to page 8 in the packet of material. Maybe they got to go to, they get teleseminars and they got to have a number to log in on the teleseminar and the DVD has to tell them they get these three teleseminars. So now if he tries to show the DVD to people without the packets, everybody's saying where's my packet? Now he can't get the packets, only they can get the packets, so now you got a way to count the cookies. If you don't have a way to count the cookies and you're getting paid per usage of anything, you will not get paid on the cookies. A lot of copywriters get paid on a number of pieces mailed. If you don't see the postal receipt, you haven't got a prayer, not a prayer, but even so, there's 600 ways to cheat with that. It's why I've never done it. The royalties they can cheat on too, but there's a lot of ways you can police that. The postage thing, not so good. Equity. You will have, and the more visible you are, the more successful you are, the more this will happen to you. You will have people who will now want to offer you pieces of their business rather than any kind of other compensation in order to help them have you as a consultant, be in your coaching programs and so forth. The worst kinds of these, of course, are the startups. I've got a great idea. I have no money. Those are the two worst things you can ever hear from anybody. The I've got a great idea is bad enough by itself. The I've got no money is bad enough by itself. The two together, really ugly. And they will want to offer you the boom. Over the years, I've done two, passed up 200. I'd be happier if I passed up 202. I suppose somebody hits a home run this way, but while you are chasing that rabbit, you're leaving a lot of money behind. Now the second kind is they come to you, they already got to go on business. They already got some numbers, and they got some kind of big expansion plan or growth plan or whatever, and now they want to offer you equity. Well, that's arguably more worth thinking about, but the biggest thing you should know about that, the short course on that is equity in a privately held company in which you are a minority owner is worthless unless and until the company is sold. And so there's a couple of things, if you insist on doing it, that you ought to think about. One is a trigger point at which the company must be sold if somebody offers X amount of dollars or at that point, if they want to turn it down, they got to give you your share of X amount of dollars. So if it's 10 million and somebody actually shows up and offers 10 million, and originally they thought they would grab that without thinking twice, but now eight years later, they're happy, they're drawing big salaries, they got a plane, they got Mercedes, they don't want the 10 million, the contract says they got to take the 10 million or they got to give you your 2 million, that's your 20% of the deal. And the second thing you want to think about is controls on, they don't get money out unless you get money out. So salaries have to be controlled, because otherwise, salaries can keep growing, payments to their mistresses can keep growing, the company cars can keep growing, and you still don't see any money. So that's not good. So equity in privately held businesses in which you are a minority partner without a lot of contractual crap that controls how they get money, how you get money, when you get money, not very useful. Perks is useful. Quite a few consulting contracts offer the opportunity for various and sundry perks. The client years ago, who did a lot of consulting for Bacardi, and Bacardi sponsors the annual penthouse photo shoot in Antigua, which is a nude photo shoot with 500 penthouse eligible women for three days. He got to go and hang out. Well, he would have worked for free, paid them, didn't make any, they're fine. So that's a nice perk.
